---
title: "Miami Blue-eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium miamiense)"
slug: "sisyrinchium-miamiense"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180783849/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Miami Blue-eyed Grass"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Asparagales"
tax_family: "Iridaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Asparagales", "Iridaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180783849/medium.jpg" alt="Miami Blue-eyed Grass" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/betseyboughton">
betseyboughton</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107482952">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Asparagales
-   **Family:** Iridaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=169023)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species Endemic to Florida.

## Natural history notes

A small, grass-like wildflower characterized by its narrow leaves and delicate blue to violet flowers with yellow centers. In Florida, it is found in moist, open habitats such as marl prairies and pine rocklands in the southern part of the state. It typically blooms in the warmer months and is most noticeable when in flower, attracting small pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/169023-Sisyrinchium-miamiense)
-   [Local profile](https://explorer.natureserve.org/Taxon/ELEMENT_GLOBAL.2.159874/Sisyrinchium_miamiense)
